title: Quiz 1 — Contracts and Abstract Data Types (Lessons 1-2)

questions.prompt: A caller violates a function's precondition and gets a wrong answer back. Whose bug is it?
choices: the implementation's — a wrong answer is always the implementation's fault
the caller's — outside its precondition, the function promises nothing
both parties', equally
the implementation's, but only because it failed to raise instead

questions.prompt: Weakening a function's precondition (accepting more inputs) makes life:
choices: easier for callers, harder for the implementer
harder for callers, easier for the implementer
easier for both sides
harder for both sides

questions.prompt: The honest, complete postcondition for sort(items) is:
choices: the output is in ascending order
the output has the same length as the input and is in ascending order
the output is in ascending order and is a permutation of the input
every element of the output appears somewhere in the input, in ascending order

questions.prompt: Running Python with -O does what to assert statements?
choices: turns assertion failures into warnings
nothing — -O only affects bytecode caching
logs assertion failures instead of raising
deletes them entirely; they never execute

questions.prompt: Because of -O, assert is the wrong tool for:
choices: validating untrusted input, like user-supplied values
spot-checking a helper's precondition during development
documenting internal invariants while debugging
expressing this-should-be-impossible conditions in draft code

questions.prompt: What defines an abstract data type?
choices: the data structure chosen to store its elements
its operations and their contracts, with the representation hidden
an abstract base class that implementations inherit from
the type annotations on its methods

questions.prompt: A client function works with ArrayStack but crashes with LinkedStack. The most likely design diagnosis:
choices: LinkedStack is an invalid implementation of the stack ADT
the stack ADT can only support one implementation at a time
the client should use isinstance to select a code path per implementation
the client depends on the representation instead of the interface
at object creation, that all interface methods exist
at import time, that classes declare the interfaces they satisfy
at each call, whether the receiver has the requested method — and no sooner
at call time, that the receiver inherits from the expected base class
